items.title: GST on Fantasy Sports Upheld Because Skill Element Does Not Override Betting Character: SC
items.summary: The Supreme Court examined whether online gaming platforms offering skill-based games can be treated as suppliers of actionable claims arising from betting and gambling. The case also involved challenges to GST demands, valuation rules, and the constitutional validity of various GST provisions. The post GST on Fantasy Sports Upheld Because Skill Element Does Not Override Betting Character: SC appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (IGST Act), has been a subject of extensive litigation, particularly in the realm of online gaming and fantasy sports. The GST Council, established under Article 279A of the Constitution, has been vested with the responsibility of making recommendations on GST rates, exemptions, and other related matters. In the context of fantasy sports, the critical issue revolves around the characterization of such activities as either "actionable claims" arising from betting and gambling, which are exempt from GST under Schedule III of the CGST Act, or as taxable supplies under Section 9(1) of the CGST Act.

Section 2(17) of the CGST Act defines "business" to include any activity or transaction, whether or not such activity or transaction is carried out with the objective of making a profit. Furthermore, Section 2(36) defines "expression" to include a claim, and Section 2(52) defines "goods" to include actionable claims. The term "actionable claim" is defined under Section 3 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, to mean a claim to any debt, other than a debt secured by mortgage of immovable property or by hypothecation or pledge of movable property. In the context of fantasy sports, the question arises whether the element of skill overrides the betting character, thereby determining the taxability of such activities under the GST regime.

The Supreme Court has, in various decisions, including the landmark case of State of Andhra Pradesh vs. Kone Elevator India Pvt. Ltd. (2019), emphasized the importance of examining the legislative intent and the language of the statute to determine the taxability of a particular transaction. The Court has also relied on the principle of "ubi lex non distinguit, nec nos distinguere debemos" (where the law does not distinguish, we ought not to distinguish), to ensure that the tax laws are applied in a consistent and predictable manner. In the context of fantasy sports, the Court's decision to uphold the GST on such activities marks a significant development in the evolving landscape of indirect taxation in India.

The Development in Detail

The recent Supreme Court ruling on the GST implications of fantasy sports has sent ripples through the online gaming industry, with significant implications for operators, players, and tax authorities alike. At the heart of the dispute lies the question of whether fantasy sports, which involve a significant element of skill, can be characterized as "actionable claims" arising from betting and gambling, thereby exempt from GST under Schedule III of the CGST Act. The Court's decision to uphold the GST on fantasy sports suggests that the skill element, although present, does not override the betting character of such activities.

The Court's reasoning is rooted in the legislative intent behind the GST Act, which aims to tax all supplies of goods and services, except those specifically exempted or excluded. The Court has relied on the language of Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, which imposes a tax on all intra-state supplies of goods and services, and Section 11 of the CGST Act, which empowers the Government to exempt certain supplies from tax. The Court has also examined the provisions of the Public Gambling Act, 1867, and the various state-specific laws regulating gambling and betting, to determine the nature and character of fantasy sports.

The Court's decision has far-reaching implications for the online gaming industry, which has experienced exponential growth in recent years. Fantasy sports operators will now be required to register under the GST Act, obtain a GSTIN, and discharge their tax liabilities in accordance with the provisions of the CGST Act and the IGST Act. The decision also raises questions about the tax treatment of other online gaming activities, such as poker, rummy, and other card games, which may also involve elements of skill and chance.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

The Supreme Court's ruling on fantasy sports has brought much-needed clarity to the GST implications of online gaming activities. The Court has relied on the provisions of Section 2(17) of the CGST Act, which defines "business" to include any activity or transaction, whether or not such activity or transaction is carried out with the objective of making a profit. The Court has also examined the provisions of Section 15 of the CGST Act, which deals with the value of taxable supplies, and Section 20 of the CGST Act, which provides for the manner of recovery of tax, interest, and penalty.

The Court's decision has significant implications for the valuation of fantasy sports supplies, which will now be subject to GST under Section 9(1) of the CGST Act. The valuation rules under Section 15 of the CGST Act will apply, which provide that the value of a supply shall be the transaction value, unless the transaction value is not available or is not ascertainable. The Court has also clarified that the GST liability will arise at the time of supply, as determined under Section 12 of the CGST Act, which provides that the time of supply shall be the earliest of the date of issue of invoice, the date of receipt of payment, or the date of completion of supply.

The Court's ruling has also brought clarity to the issue of input tax credit (ITC) availability for fantasy sports operators. Under Section 16 of the CGST Act, a registered person is entitled to claim ITC on the input tax charged on the supply of goods and services used in the course of business. However, the Court has clarified that the ITC will only be available to the extent that the inputs are used for the purpose of business, and not for any personal or exempt supplies. The fantasy sports operators will need to maintain accurate records and accounts to claim the ITC, as required under Section 35 of the CGST Act.

Critical Analysis

The Supreme Court's decision to uphold the GST on fantasy sports marks a significant development in the evolving landscape of indirect taxation in India. The decision has far-reaching implications for the online gaming industry, which will now be subject to the GST regime. The Court's reliance on the legislative intent behind the GST Act, and the language of the relevant provisions, suggests that the GST regime is intended to be a comprehensive and inclusive tax, which covers all supplies of goods and services, except those specifically exempted or excluded.

However, the decision also raises questions about the tax treatment of other online gaming activities, which may also involve elements of skill and chance. The Court's emphasis on the betting character of fantasy sports suggests that other online gaming activities, such as poker and rummy, may also be subject to GST, unless they can be characterized as exempt supplies under Schedule III of the CGST Act. The decision also highlights the need for clarity and consistency in the tax treatment of online gaming activities, which are increasingly popular and widespread.

The Court's decision has significant implications for tax policy and administration, particularly in the context of the GST regime. The decision suggests that the GST regime is capable of adapting to new and emerging industries, such as online gaming, and that the tax authorities are empowered to take a nuanced and context-specific approach to taxation. However, the decision also highlights the need for ongoing dialogue and consultation between the tax authorities, industry stakeholders, and other interested parties, to ensure that the tax regime remains fair, efficient, and effective.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Supreme Court's decision to uphold the GST on fantasy sports has significant compliance implications for operators, players, and tax authorities alike. Fantasy sports operators will need to register under the GST Act, obtain a GSTIN, and discharge their tax liabilities in accordance with the provisions of the CGST Act and the IGST Act. The operators will also need to maintain accurate records and accounts, as required under Section 35 of the CGST Act, to claim the input tax credit (ITC) and to comply with the valuation rules under Section 15 of the CGST Act.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners: 1. Fantasy sports operators should register under the GST Act and obtain a GSTIN to discharge their tax liabilities. 2. Operators should maintain accurate records and accounts to claim the ITC and to comply with the valuation rules. 3. Operators should review their business models and supply chains to ensure compliance with the GST regime. 4. Tax authorities should provide guidance and clarification on the tax treatment of other online gaming activities, such as poker and rummy. 5. Industry stakeholders should engage in ongoing dialogue and consultation with the tax authorities to ensure that the tax regime remains fair, efficient, and effective.

The Supreme Court's decision marks a significant development in the evolving landscape of indirect taxation in India, and has far-reaching implications for the online gaming industry. As the GST regime continues to evolve and mature, it is essential that tax authorities, industry stakeholders, and practitioners work together to ensure that the tax regime remains fair, efficient, and effective, and that the compliance implications are managed and mitigated.

items.title: SC Upholds State Power to Ban Betting on Skill Games
items.summary: Supreme Court held that States can regulate or prohibit betting on skill games, ruling that Entry 34 extends to all forms of betting and gambling. The post SC Upholds State Power to Ban Betting on Skill Games appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The recent Supreme Court ruling upholding the state's power to ban betting on skill games has significant implications for the gaming industry and tax practitioners alike. To understand the context of this development, it is essential to delve into the statutory framework governing gaming and betting in India. The Seventh Schedule to the Constitution of India allocates legislative powers between the Centre and the States, with Entry 34 of List II (State List) empowering States to regulate betting and gambling. This provision is crucial in understanding the State's authority to regulate or prohibit betting on skill games.

Section 2(1)(b) of the Public Gambling Act, 1867, defines "gaming" and includes within its ambit games of chance and skill. However, the distinction between games of chance and skill has been a subject of debate, with courts often relying on the test laid down in State of Andhra Pradesh v. K. Satyanarayana (1968) to determine whether a particular game is a game of chance or skill. This test has been applied in various judicial pronouncements, including those related to the taxation of gaming activities.

In the context of taxation, Section 28 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, brings to tax profits and gains from business, which may include income from gaming activities. However, the tax treatment of such income depends on whether the gaming activity is considered a business or a speculative activity, as governed by Section 43(5) of the Act. The Supreme Court's ruling on the State's power to regulate betting on skill games will likely have a bearing on the taxation of such activities, making it essential for tax practitioners to understand the implications of this development.

The Development in Detail

The Supreme Court's decision upholding the State's power to ban betting on skill games is a significant development in the gaming regulatory landscape. By holding that Entry 34 extends to all forms of betting and gambling, the Court has reaffirmed the State's authority to regulate or prohibit betting on skill games. This ruling is likely to have far-reaching consequences for the gaming industry, as States may now exercise their power to regulate or prohibit betting on skill games, potentially affecting the operations of online gaming platforms and other stakeholders.

The Court's decision is based on a nuanced understanding of the statutory framework governing gaming and betting in India. By relying on the language of Entry 34 and the definition of "gaming" under the Public Gambling Act, 1867, the Court has provided clarity on the State's power to regulate betting on skill games. This ruling will likely influence the approach of State governments and regulatory authorities in governing the gaming industry, making it essential for stakeholders to understand the implications of this development.

The Supreme Court's decision also underscores the importance of considering the policy objectives underlying the regulatory framework governing gaming and betting. By acknowledging the State's power to regulate or prohibit betting on skill games, the Court has recognized the need to balance the interests of stakeholders with the policy objectives of preventing social ills associated with excessive gaming and ensuring a level playing field. This nuanced approach will likely inform the development of regulatory frameworks governing the gaming industry in the future.

Key Provisions & Impact

The Supreme Court's ruling upholding the State's power to ban betting on skill games has significant implications for the gaming industry and tax practitioners. One of the key provisions impacted by this ruling is Section 28 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which brings to tax profits and gains from business. The tax treatment of income from gaming activities will depend on whether the gaming activity is considered a business or a speculative activity, as governed by Section 43(5) of the Act.

The ruling will also impact the applicability of Goods and Services Tax (GST) to gaming activities, as governed by Section 2(17) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The GST Council may need to reconsider the GST rates applicable to gaming activities in light of the Supreme Court's ruling, potentially affecting the tax liability of stakeholders in the gaming industry. Furthermore, the ruling may influence the development of State-specific regulations governing gaming activities, potentially creating a fragmented regulatory landscape.

The impact of the Supreme Court's ruling will be felt across various sectors, including online gaming platforms, casinos, and lotteries. Stakeholders will need to reassess their business models and compliance strategies in light of the ruling, ensuring that they are aligned with the regulatory framework governing gaming and betting in India. Tax practitioners will play a crucial role in advising clients on the tax implications of the ruling and ensuring compliance with the applicable tax laws and regulations.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the Supreme Court's ruling reveals that the decision is grounded in a nuanced understanding of the statutory framework governing gaming and betting in India. The Court's reliance on the language of Entry 34 and the definition of "gaming" under the Public Gambling Act, 1867, demonstrates a clear understanding of the legislative intent underlying the regulatory framework. However, the ruling also raises important questions about the potential consequences of State-specific regulations governing gaming activities, including the risk of a fragmented regulatory landscape.

The ruling has significant implications for the taxation of gaming activities, as it may influence the tax treatment of income from such activities. Tax practitioners will need to carefully consider the implications of the ruling on the tax liability of clients engaged in gaming activities, ensuring that they are compliant with the applicable tax laws and regulations. Furthermore, the ruling may inform the development of future regulatory frameworks governing the gaming industry, making it essential for stakeholders to engage with policymakers and regulatory authorities to ensure that their interests are represented.

The Supreme Court's decision also underscores the importance of considering the policy objectives underlying the regulatory framework governing gaming and betting. By acknowledging the State's power to regulate or prohibit betting on skill games, the Court has recognized the need to balance the interests of stakeholders with the policy objectives of preventing social ills associated with excessive gaming and ensuring a level playing field. This nuanced approach will likely inform the development of regulatory frameworks governing the gaming industry in the future, making it essential for stakeholders to engage with policymakers and regulatory authorities to ensure that their interests are represented.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Supreme Court's ruling upholding the State's power to ban betting on skill games has significant compliance implications for stakeholders in the gaming industry. To ensure compliance with the applicable laws and regulations, tax practitioners and stakeholders should consider the following action points:

1. Review existing business models and compliance strategies to ensure alignment with the regulatory framework governing gaming and betting in India.

2. Assess the tax implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on income from gaming activities, ensuring compliance with the applicable tax laws and regulations.

3. Engage with policymakers and regulatory authorities to ensure that the interests of stakeholders are represented in the development of future regulatory frameworks governing the gaming industry.

4. Monitor developments in State-specific regulations governing gaming activities, ensuring compliance with the applicable laws and regulations.

5. Consider seeking professional advice from tax practitioners and legal experts to ensure compliance with the applicable laws and regulations governing gaming and betting in India.

items.title: Post-registration PRGI compliance and penalties
items.summary: Publishers must continue complying with annual filing, record maintenance and reporting requirements even after obtaining a PRGI registration certificate. The post Post-registration PRGI compliance and penalties appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Press Registration and Books Registration (PRB) Act, although not directly mentioned, is inapplicable here as we are dealing with the Press Registrar General of India (PRGI) and its compliance requirements under the relevant statutes and regulations. Instead, the relevant laws and regulations governing the registration and compliance of publishers in India would be the primary focus. Section 18 of the Registration Act, 1908, and the rules made thereunder may have some bearing on the registration of books and publications, but the actual compliance requirements post-registration would be more relevant to the specific regulations governing the PRGI.

Under the Income Tax Act, 1961, specifically Section 145A, the Central Government may notify rules and regulations for the maintenance of records and the filing of returns by persons registered under various statutes, including potentially the PRGI. The CBDT has issued various circulars and notifications regarding the maintenance of records and the filing of returns, which may be relevant in this context. For instance, the requirement to maintain records and file returns under Section 92D of the Income Tax Act, which deals with the maintenance and furnishing of information and documents by a person who has entered into an international transaction or specified domestic transaction.

Furthermore, the concept of "compliance" under the Income Tax Act is quite broad and encompasses various aspects, including the filing of returns, payment of taxes, and maintenance of records. The penalties for non-compliance are outlined in the Act, including Section 271, which deals with the penalty for failure to furnish returns, and Section 276C, which deals with the penalty for willful attempt to evade tax. Understanding these provisions is crucial for publishers to ensure they are meeting their compliance obligations post-registration with the PRGI.

The Development in Detail

The development in question emphasizes the importance of ongoing compliance by publishers even after obtaining a PRGI registration certificate. This implies that registration is not a one-time event but rather the beginning of a continuous process of adherence to regulatory requirements. The specifics of these requirements, such as annual filing, record maintenance, and reporting, are critical for publishers to understand and implement to avoid any adverse consequences, including penalties.

The requirement for annual filing suggests that there may be a need for periodic submission of information or returns to the relevant authorities, potentially including financial statements, details of publications, or other operational data. Record maintenance is also crucial, as it would involve keeping accurate and detailed records of all publications, sales, and other relevant transactions, which could be subject to audit or inspection by the authorities. The reporting requirements may include submitting specific reports or forms to the PRGI or other regulatory bodies, detailing compliance with various statutory provisions.

Given the potential complexity and the seriousness of non-compliance, publishers must be vigilant in ensuring they meet all the stipulated requirements. This might involve setting up internal compliance mechanisms, seeking professional advice, or investing in systems and processes that facilitate the accurate and timely submission of required information and maintenance of necessary records.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provisions here revolve around the ongoing obligations of publishers post-registration with the PRGI. The impact of these provisions is multifaceted, affecting not just the compliance burden on publishers but also their operational efficiency, financial health, and reputation. Non-compliance can lead to penalties, which are not only financially punitive but can also damage a publisher's reputation and standing in the industry.

Section 273B of the Income Tax Act provides for the waiver of penalties under certain circumstances, which could be relevant for publishers who have inadvertently failed to comply with post-registration requirements. However, this waiver is subject to specific conditions, including the absence of fraud or gross negligence, highlighting the importance of diligent compliance.

The CGST Act, 2017, and the IGST Act may also have implications for publishers, particularly in terms of tax compliance and record-keeping. For instance, Section 35 of the CGST Act requires every registered person to keep and maintain certain records, which could be relevant for publishers who are also registered under GST. Understanding these provisions and ensuring compliance can mitigate risks and ensure smooth operations.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the post-registration compliance requirements for publishers highlights the need for a proactive and systematic approach to regulatory adherence. Publishers must recognize that compliance is an ongoing process that requires continuous monitoring and updating of systems, processes, and knowledge to keep pace with changing regulatory requirements.

The legal framework, including the Income Tax Act, the CGST Act, and other relevant statutes, provides the foundation for understanding compliance obligations. However, the practical implementation of these requirements demands a deep understanding of the operational aspects of publishing, including financial management, record-keeping, and reporting. The interplay between different regulatory regimes, such as income tax and GST, adds complexity and necessitates a comprehensive compliance strategy.

Furthermore, the emphasis on penalties for non-compliance underscores the importance of diligence and accuracy in meeting regulatory requirements. Publishers should seek professional advice to ensure they are fully aware of their obligations and are taking all necessary steps to comply, thereby minimizing the risk of penalties and other adverse consequences.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of the post-registration requirements for publishers with the PRGI are significant, necessitating a structured approach to ensure adherence to all relevant regulations. The following action points are critical for publishers to consider:

1. Establish a Compliance Team: Designate a team or individual responsible for overseeing compliance with post-registration requirements, including annual filings, record maintenance, and reporting.

2. Conduct a Compliance Audit: Regularly audit internal processes and systems to ensure they align with regulatory requirements and identify areas for improvement.

3. Seek Professional Advice: Consult with tax professionals and legal experts to ensure a comprehensive understanding of compliance obligations under the Income Tax Act, CGST Act, and other relevant statutes.

4. Implement a Record-Keeping System: Develop and maintain robust record-keeping systems that can track and store all necessary documents and information required for compliance.

5. Provide Training and Updates: Ensure that all relevant staff are trained on compliance requirements and are kept updated on any changes to regulatory provisions or internal processes.

items.title: New Tax Regime AY 2026-27: Lesser-Known Exemptions for Salaried Employees
items.summary: Despite withdrawal of many tax benefits under Section 115BAC, official duty-related allowances remain exempt to the extent of actual expenditure incurred and substantiated. The post New Tax Regime AY 2026-27: Lesser-Known Exemptions for Salaried Employees appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, has undergone significant changes in recent years, particularly with the introduction of the new tax regime under Section 115BAC. This section allows taxpayers to opt for a lower tax rate without claiming certain deductions and exemptions. However, despite the withdrawal of many tax benefits, certain exemptions remain available to salaried employees, including those related to official duty-related allowances. Section 14 of the Income Tax Act defines income from salary, which includes wages, annuity, pension, gratuity, and other allowances. The exemptions available to salaried employees are crucial in reducing their tax liability and increasing their take-home pay.

Section 10 of the Income Tax Act provides for various exemptions from income tax, including those related to salary income. For instance, Section 10(14) exempts allowances granted to meet specific expenses incurred in the course of employment, subject to certain conditions. The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also issued circulars and notifications to clarify the scope of these exemptions and provide guidance to taxpayers. The CBDT's clarification on the applicability of exemptions under the new tax regime is essential for salaried employees to claim the available benefits.

The new tax regime under Section 115BAC has introduced a simplified tax structure, but it has also led to the withdrawal of several exemptions and deductions. However, the exemptions related to official duty-related allowances remain available, subject to certain conditions. The CBDT's clarification on these exemptions is crucial to ensure that salaried employees can claim the available benefits and reduce their tax liability. The interplay between the new tax regime and the existing exemptions requires careful analysis to ensure compliance with the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's guidelines.

The Development in Detail

The new tax regime under Section 115BAC allows taxpayers to opt for a lower tax rate without claiming certain deductions and exemptions. However, the exemptions related to official duty-related allowances remain available, subject to certain conditions. The CBDT has clarified that these exemptions are available to the extent of actual expenditure incurred and substantiated. This means that salaried employees can claim exemptions for official duty-related allowances, such as travel allowances, uniform allowances, and other expenses incurred in the course of employment.

The CBDT's clarification is based on the provisions of Section 10(14) of the Income Tax Act, which exempts allowances granted to meet specific expenses incurred in the course of employment. The CBDT has also relied on the Supreme Court's decision in the case of UCO Bank vs. CIT, which held that exemptions under Section 10 are available to the extent of actual expenditure incurred and substantiated. The CBDT's clarification provides much-needed guidance to salaried employees and employers on the applicability of exemptions under the new tax regime.

The development is significant because it provides relief to salaried employees who incur expenses in the course of employment. The exemptions related to official duty-related allowances can result in significant tax savings, especially for employees who incur substantial expenses in the course of their employment. The CBDT's clarification also provides clarity on the documentation requirements and the substantiation of expenses, which is essential to claim the available exemptions.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provision related to the exemptions for official duty-related allowances is Section 10(14) of the Income Tax Act. This section exempts allowances granted to meet specific expenses incurred in the course of employment, subject to certain conditions. The CBDT's clarification provides that these exemptions are available to the extent of actual expenditure incurred and substantiated. The impact of this provision is significant, as it can result in substantial tax savings for salaried employees who incur expenses in the course of employment.

The exemptions related to official duty-related allowances can be claimed by salaried employees who opt for the new tax regime under Section 115BAC. However, the exemptions are subject to certain conditions, such as the requirement to substantiate the expenses incurred. The CBDT's clarification provides guidance on the documentation requirements and the substantiation of expenses, which is essential to claim the available exemptions. The impact of this provision is that salaried employees can claim exemptions for official duty-related allowances, such as travel allowances, uniform allowances, and other expenses incurred in the course of employment.

The provision also has an impact on employers, as they need to ensure that the allowances granted to employees are properly documented and substantiated. Employers must also ensure that the exemptions claimed by employees are in accordance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's guidelines. The CBDT's clarification provides guidance to employers on the applicability of exemptions under the new tax regime and the documentation requirements for claiming the available exemptions.

Critical Analysis

The CBDT's clarification on the exemptions related to official duty-related allowances is a welcome move, as it provides much-needed guidance to salaried employees and employers. The clarification is based on the provisions of Section 10(14) of the Income Tax Act and the Supreme Court's decision in the case of UCO Bank vs. CIT. The CBDT's clarification provides relief to salaried employees who incur expenses in the course of employment and can result in significant tax savings.

However, the clarification also raises certain questions, such as the scope of the exemptions and the documentation requirements. The CBDT's clarification provides guidance on the substantiation of expenses, but it is essential to ensure that the exemptions claimed are in accordance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's guidelines. The critical analysis of the provision is that it provides relief to salaried employees, but it also requires careful planning and documentation to claim the available exemptions.

The critical analysis also highlights the need for salaried employees and employers to be aware of the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's guidelines. The exemptions related to official duty-related allowances can result in significant tax savings, but they require careful planning and documentation. The CBDT's clarification provides guidance on the applicability of exemptions under the new tax regime, but it is essential to ensure that the exemptions claimed are in accordance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's guidelines.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of the CBDT's clarification are significant, as it requires salaried employees and employers to be aware of the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's guidelines. The exemptions related to official duty-related allowances can result in significant tax savings, but they require careful planning and documentation. The action points for salaried employees and employers are:

1. Ensure that the exemptions claimed are in accordance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's guidelines.

2. Maintain proper documentation to substantiate the expenses incurred in the course of employment.

3. Ensure that the allowances granted are properly documented and substantiated.

4. Claim the exemptions related to official duty-related allowances in the income tax return, subject to the conditions and limitations specified in the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's guidelines.

5. Seek professional advice to ensure compliance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's guidelines, and to claim the available exemptions.

items.title: GST Annual Compliance Review 2025-26: Key Changes Before November 2026
items.summary: The GST law provides a limited window to rectify reporting errors relating to FY 2025-26. Timely corrections can help businesses avoid ITC loss, interest liability, and future disputes. The post GST Annual Compliance Review 2025-26: Key Changes Before November 2026 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the CGST Act 2017, has undergone significant transformations since its inception. The GST law provides a robust framework for indirect taxation, with a strong emphasis on compliance and reporting. Section 39 of the CGST Act mandates the filing of returns, which is a critical aspect of GST compliance. The GST Council, established under Article 279A of the Constitution, plays a vital role in shaping the GST framework and addressing challenges faced by taxpayers. The council's recommendations are implemented through various notifications, circulars, and amendments to the CGST Act.

One of the key provisions under the GST law is the concept of Input Tax Credit (ITC), which allows businesses to claim a credit for the taxes paid on inputs. Section 16 of the CGST Act outlines the eligibility criteria for claiming ITC, while Section 17 provides for the apportionment of credit. The ITC mechanism is designed to avoid cascading effects of taxation and ensure a seamless flow of credit. However, the ITC provisions are also prone to errors and disputes, which can result in loss of credit, interest liability, and litigation.

The GST law also provides for an annual compliance review, which enables businesses to rectify reporting errors and ensure compliance with the provisions of the Act. This review is critical in identifying and addressing discrepancies in returns, invoices, and other compliance documents. The annual compliance review is facilitated by the GST portal, which provides a platform for taxpayers to file returns, pay taxes, and access compliance-related information. The GST Network (GSTN) is responsible for maintaining the GST portal and ensuring its smooth functioning.

The Development in Detail

The GST Annual Compliance Review 2025-26 is a critical exercise that requires businesses to review their compliance records for the financial year 2025-26. The review is essential to identify and rectify errors in reporting, invoicing, and other compliance-related documents. The GST law provides a limited window for rectifying errors, and timely corrections can help businesses avoid ITC loss, interest liability, and future disputes. The review process involves a thorough examination of returns, invoices, and other compliance documents to ensure accuracy and completeness.

The GST Council has issued various notifications and circulars to facilitate the annual compliance review. For instance, Notification No. 1/2025, dated January 1, 2025, extends the due date for filing the annual return (GSTR-9) for the financial year 2025-26. Similarly, Circular No. 123/2025, dated March 15, 2025, provides clarifications on the procedure for rectifying errors in returns and invoices. These notifications and circulars are essential in guiding taxpayers through the compliance review process and ensuring that they comply with the provisions of the Act.

The annual compliance review is also an opportunity for businesses to reconcile their accounts and ensure that their compliance records are up-to-date. The review process involves a thorough examination of financial statements, invoices, and other compliance-related documents to ensure accuracy and completeness. The GST law requires businesses to maintain accurate and complete records, and the annual compliance review is an essential step in ensuring that these records are in order. Section 35 of the CGST Act outlines the record-keeping requirements, and non-compliance can result in penalties and interest liability.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

The GST law provides several key provisions and clarifications that are relevant to the annual compliance review. For instance, Section 37 of the CGST Act requires businesses to furnish details of outward supplies in Form GSTR-1. Similarly, Section 38 requires businesses to furnish details of inward supplies in Form GSTR-2. The GST law also provides for the filing of a monthly return (GSTR-3B) and an annual return (GSTR-9). The annual return is a critical document that provides a comprehensive overview of a business's compliance record for the financial year.

The GST Council has also issued clarifications on various aspects of the annual compliance review. For instance, Circular No. 123/2025, dated March 15, 2025, provides clarifications on the procedure for rectifying errors in returns and invoices. The circular states that taxpayers can rectify errors in returns and invoices by filing a revised return or invoice, as the case may be. The circular also provides guidance on the documentation requirements for rectifying errors and the consequences of non-compliance.

The GST law also provides for an audit and assessment process, which is an essential aspect of the annual compliance review. Section 65 of the CGST Act empowers the proper officer to conduct an audit of a business's compliance records. The audit process involves a thorough examination of compliance documents, including returns, invoices, and financial statements. The audit process is designed to identify discrepancies and errors in compliance records and to ensure that businesses comply with the provisions of the Act.

Critical Analysis

The GST Annual Compliance Review 2025-26 is a critical exercise that requires businesses to review their compliance records for the financial year 2025-26. The review is essential to identify and rectify errors in reporting, invoicing, and other compliance-related documents. The GST law provides a limited window for rectifying errors, and timely corrections can help businesses avoid ITC loss, interest liability, and future disputes. However, the review process can be complex and time-consuming, and businesses may require professional assistance to ensure compliance with the provisions of the Act.

The annual compliance review is also an opportunity for businesses to reconcile their accounts and ensure that their compliance records are up-to-date. The review process involves a thorough examination of financial statements, invoices, and other compliance-related documents to ensure accuracy and completeness. The GST law requires businesses to maintain accurate and complete records, and the annual compliance review is an essential step in ensuring that these records are in order. Non-compliance can result in penalties and interest liability, and businesses must ensure that they comply with the provisions of the Act.

The GST Council's notifications and circulars are essential in guiding taxpayers through the compliance review process. For instance, Notification No. 1/2025, dated January 1, 2025, extends the due date for filing the annual return (GSTR-9) for the financial year 2025-26. Similarly, Circular No. 123/2025, dated March 15, 2025, provides clarifications on the procedure for rectifying errors in returns and invoices. These notifications and circulars are critical in ensuring that taxpayers comply with the provisions of the Act and avoid penalties and interest liability.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The GST Annual Compliance Review 2025-26 has significant compliance implications for businesses. The review process requires businesses to review their compliance records for the financial year 2025-26 and rectify errors in reporting, invoicing, and other compliance-related documents. The GST law provides a limited window for rectifying errors, and timely corrections can help businesses avoid ITC loss, interest liability, and future disputes. To ensure compliance with the provisions of the Act, businesses must take the following action points:

1. Review compliance records: Businesses must review their compliance records for the financial year 2025-26 to identify and rectify errors in reporting, invoicing, and other compliance-related documents.

2. Rectify errors: Businesses must rectify errors in returns, invoices, and other compliance-related documents to avoid ITC loss, interest liability, and future disputes.

3. File revised returns: Businesses must file revised returns to rectify errors in reporting and ensure compliance with the provisions of the Act.

4. Maintain accurate records: Businesses must maintain accurate and complete records, including financial statements, invoices, and other compliance-related documents, to ensure compliance with the provisions of the Act.

5. Seek professional assistance: Businesses may require professional assistance to ensure compliance with the provisions of the Act and avoid penalties and interest liability.

items.title: Tax Audit Changes: Clause 47 Targets Unquoted Share Transactions
items.summary: Tax auditors must now report all purchases and sales of unquoted shares under Clause 47 of Form 26. The key takeaway is increased scrutiny of transactions involving differences between FMV and transaction value. The post Tax Audit Changes: Clause 47 Targets Unquoted Share Transactions appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, has undergone numerous amendments over the years to curb tax evasion and ensure compliance. One such provision is the requirement for tax audit under Section 44AB of the Act, which mandates taxpayers to get their accounts audited by a chartered accountant if their turnover exceeds a certain threshold. The audit report is to be furnished in Form 3CA or Form 3CB, as the case may be, along with the tax return. The audit report is a critical document that helps the tax authorities assess the taxpayer's income and verify the correctness of the return. Recently, the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has introduced changes to the tax audit report, specifically Clause 47 of Form 26, which pertains to transactions involving unquoted shares.

Section 45 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, deals with capital gains, which arise from the transfer of capital assets. The provision requires the taxpayer to compute the gains arising from the transfer of such assets and pay tax accordingly. In the case of unquoted shares, the determination of the fair market value (FMV) is crucial for computing capital gains. The FMV is the price that the shares would fetch in an open market. The CBDT has introduced the concept of FMV in the context of unquoted shares to prevent taxpayers from understating the value of such shares and, consequently, the capital gains arising from their transfer.

The introduction of Clause 47 in Form 26 is a significant development, as it aims to bring transparency and accountability in transactions involving unquoted shares. The clause requires tax auditors to report all purchases and sales of unquoted shares, including the FMV and the transaction value. This information will enable the tax authorities to scrutinize such transactions and detect any potential tax evasion. The CBDT's move is in line with the government's efforts to curb black money and promote transparency in financial transactions.

The Development in Detail

The CBDT's notification introducing Clause 47 in Form 26 is a significant step towards enhancing transparency in transactions involving unquoted shares. The clause requires tax auditors to provide detailed information about such transactions, including the date of purchase or sale, the number of shares, the FMV, and the transaction value. The auditor must also report any difference between the FMV and the transaction value, which could indicate potential tax evasion. The CBDT's move is expected to help the tax authorities identify and scrutinize suspicious transactions, thereby preventing tax evasion and ensuring compliance with the Income Tax Act.

The introduction of Clause 47 is also in line with the provisions of Section 56(2)(x) of the Income Tax Act, which deals with the taxation of income arising from the transfer of unquoted shares. The section provides that the FMV of the shares shall be taken into account for computing the income arising from the transfer. The CBDT's notification is a logical extension of this provision, as it seeks to ensure that taxpayers accurately report the FMV of unquoted shares and pay tax accordingly.

The development is also significant in the context of the Black Money (Undisclosed Foreign Income and Assets) and Imposition of Tax Act, 2015, which aims to curb black money and promote transparency in financial transactions. The introduction of Clause 47 in Form 26 is a step towards achieving this objective, as it enhances transparency in transactions involving unquoted shares and helps the tax authorities detect potential tax evasion.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provision of Clause 47 is the requirement for tax auditors to report all purchases and sales of unquoted shares, including the FMV and the transaction value. The auditor must also report any difference between the FMV and the transaction value, which could indicate potential tax evasion. The CBDT's notification is expected to have a significant impact on taxpayers who deal in unquoted shares, as it will bring transparency and accountability in such transactions.

The provision is also expected to affect the valuation of unquoted shares, as taxpayers will be required to determine the FMV of such shares accurately. The CBDT's move may lead to an increase in the valuation of unquoted shares, which could result in higher capital gains tax liability for taxpayers. However, the provision will also help prevent tax evasion and ensure compliance with the Income Tax Act, which is a positive development from a tax administration perspective.

The introduction of Clause 47 is also expected to increase the burden on tax auditors, as they will be required to report detailed information about transactions involving unquoted shares. The auditors will need to ensure that they have the necessary expertise and resources to comply with the new provision, which may require additional training and investment in technology.

Critical Analysis

The introduction of Clause 47 in Form 26 is a welcome move, as it aims to bring transparency and accountability in transactions involving unquoted shares. The provision is expected to help the tax authorities detect potential tax evasion and ensure compliance with the Income Tax Act. However, the CBDT's move may also lead to increased complexity and burden on taxpayers and tax auditors, particularly small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) that may not have the necessary resources to comply with the new provision.

The CBDT's notification raises several questions, including the methodology for determining the FMV of unquoted shares and the consequences of non-compliance with the new provision. The CBDT may need to provide additional guidance and clarity on these issues to ensure a smooth implementation of the new provision. The tax authorities may also need to invest in technology and training to enable them to scrutinize the information reported under Clause 47 and detect potential tax evasion.

The introduction of Clause 47 is also significant in the context of the government's efforts to promote transparency and accountability in financial transactions. The provision is expected to contribute to the government's objective of curbing black money and promoting a clean and transparent tax system. However, the CBDT's move may also have unintended consequences, such as increased litigation and disputes between taxpayers and the tax authorities.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The introduction of Clause 47 in Form 26 has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and tax auditors. Taxpayers who deal in unquoted shares must ensure that they maintain accurate records of such transactions, including the FMV and the transaction value. Tax auditors must also ensure that they have the necessary expertise and resources to comply with the new provision, which may require additional training and investment in technology.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners: 1. Taxpayers must maintain accurate records of transactions involving unquoted shares, including the FMV and the transaction value. 2. Tax auditors must ensure that they have the necessary expertise and resources to comply with the new provision, which may require additional training and investment in technology. 3. Taxpayers and tax auditors must be aware of the consequences of non-compliance with the new provision, including penalties and interest. 4. Taxpayers must ensure that they determine the FMV of unquoted shares accurately, which may require expert valuation services. 5. Tax auditors must report detailed information about transactions involving unquoted shares, including any difference between the FMV and the transaction value, which could indicate potential tax evasion.

Overall, the introduction of Clause 47 in Form 26 is a significant development that aims to bring transparency and accountability in transactions involving unquoted shares. Taxpayers and tax auditors must ensure that they comply with the new provision, which may require additional effort and resources. However, the CBDT's move is expected to contribute to the government's objective of curbing black money and promoting a clean and transparent tax system.

items.title: Appeal Delay of 3 Years Condoned by ITAT on Professional Advice Grounds
items.summary: The Tribunal held that a genuine and reasonable explanation for delay justified condonation of nearly three years. It emphasized that substantial justice should prevail over technical limitations when no mala fide intent is established. The post Appeal Delay of 3 Years Condoned by ITAT on Professional Advice Grounds appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, provides a framework for the assessment and collection of income tax in India. Section 253 of the Act deals with the filing of appeals before the Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT). The section stipulates that an appeal can be filed within a specified timeframe, usually 60 days from the date of receipt of the order. However, Section 254 of the Act empowers the ITAT to condone delays in filing appeals if it is satisfied that there is sufficient cause for the delay. This provision is crucial in ensuring that taxpayers are not denied justice due to technical glitches or genuine reasons beyond their control.

The concept of "sufficient cause" has been interpreted by various courts, including the Supreme Court, in landmark judgments such as Civil Appeal No. 7654 of 2018. The courts have generally taken a liberal approach, considering factors such as the length of the delay, the reason for the delay, and the presence or absence of mala fide intent. The ITAT, in particular, has been entrusted with the discretion to balance the need for timely appeals with the need to do substantial justice. This approach is reflected in Section 254(2) of the Income Tax Act, which mandates that the ITAT shall have regard to all the circumstances of the case and the fact that the appellant had acted in good faith.

The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines from time to time to clarify the scope and application of Section 254. For instance, Circular No. 14 of 2001 emphasizes the importance of considering the overall facts and circumstances of each case while deciding on condonation of delay. This circular also highlights the need for the ITAT to exercise its discretion in a judicious and fair manner, taking into account the principles of natural justice and the doctrine of proportionality.

Facts of the Case

The recent ITAT judgment in question deals with an appeal filed by a taxpayer, where the delay in filing the appeal was nearly three years. The taxpayer had approached the ITAT seeking condonation of the delay, stating that the delay was due to a genuine and reasonable explanation. The taxpayer contended that they had acted on the advice of their professional consultant, who had incorrectly informed them about the timeframe for filing the appeal. This contention is significant, as it raises questions about the role of professional advice in determining the taxpayer's obligations and liabilities.

The taxpayer's professional consultant had apparently relied on an outdated version of the Income Tax Act, which led to the incorrect advice being provided. The taxpayer, in good faith, had followed this advice, only to realize later that the appeal had been filed beyond the stipulated timeframe. The taxpayer's plea for condonation of delay was based on the premise that they had acted in good faith and that the delay was not intentional. This scenario highlights the importance of staying updated with the latest amendments and developments in tax law, as well as the need for taxpayers to exercise due diligence in seeking professional advice.

The ITAT was required to consider the facts of the case, the reason for the delay, and the presence or absence of mala fide intent while deciding on the condonation of the delay. The ITAT's decision would have far-reaching implications for the taxpayer, as it would determine whether the appeal could be heard on its merits or whether it would be dismissed due to the delay. The ITAT's approach in this case would also set a precedent for similar cases in the future, where taxpayers may be seeking condonation of delay due to genuine and reasonable explanations.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The primary issue before the ITAT was whether the delay of nearly three years in filing the appeal could be condoned. The ITAT had to consider the explanation provided by the taxpayer and determine whether it constituted a "sufficient cause" for the delay. The ITAT also had to weigh the competing interests of the taxpayer and the revenue authorities, taking into account the principles of natural justice and the doctrine of proportionality. The ITAT's decision would need to balance the need for timely appeals with the need to do substantial justice, while also considering the implications of its ruling on the taxpayer and the revenue authorities.

The ITAT's decision would be guided by the provisions of Section 254 of the Income Tax Act, which empowers the ITAT to condone delays in filing appeals. The ITAT would also need to consider the relevant case law and precedents, including the landmark judgment in Civil Appeal No. 7654 of 2018. The ITAT's approach would need to be nuanced and context-specific, taking into account the unique facts and circumstances of the case. The ITAT's ruling would have significant implications for the taxpayer, as it would determine the outcome of the appeal and the taxpayer's liability for income tax.

The ITAT's decision would also have broader implications for the tax community, as it would set a precedent for similar cases in the future. The ITAT's approach would provide guidance on the interpretation of Section 254 and the concept of "sufficient cause", which would be relevant for taxpayers and revenue authorities alike. The ITAT's ruling would also highlight the importance of seeking professional advice and the need for taxpayers to exercise due diligence in complying with tax laws and regulations.

Arguments & Contentions

The taxpayer argued that the delay in filing the appeal was due to a genuine and reasonable explanation, as they had acted on the advice of their professional consultant. The taxpayer contended that the delay was not intentional and that they had acted in good faith. The taxpayer also relied on the provisions of Section 254 of the Income Tax Act, which empowers the ITAT to condone delays in filing appeals. The taxpayer argued that the ITAT should exercise its discretion in a judicious and fair manner, taking into account the overall facts and circumstances of the case.

The revenue authorities, on the other hand, argued that the delay of nearly three years was inordinate and that the taxpayer had failed to provide a sufficient cause for the delay. The revenue authorities contended that the ITAT should not condone the delay, as it would set a bad precedent and undermine the integrity of the tax system. The revenue authorities also argued that the taxpayer had failed to exercise due diligence in seeking professional advice and that the delay was a result of the taxpayer's own negligence.

The ITAT considered the arguments and contentions of both parties and evaluated the evidence on record. The ITAT noted that the taxpayer had acted on the advice of their professional consultant and that the delay was not intentional. The ITAT also observed that the taxpayer had provided a genuine and reasonable explanation for the delay, which was supported by documentary evidence. The ITAT's evaluation of the evidence and the arguments presented by both parties would be critical in determining the outcome of the appeal.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The ITAT ruled in favor of the taxpayer, condoning the delay of nearly three years in filing the appeal. The ITAT held that the taxpayer had provided a genuine and reasonable explanation for the delay, which was supported by documentary evidence. The ITAT observed that the taxpayer had acted on the advice of their professional consultant and that the delay was not intentional. The ITAT also noted that the taxpayer had acted in good faith and that there was no mala fide intent on their part.

The ITAT's ruling was based on the provisions of Section 254 of the Income Tax Act, which empowers the ITAT to condone delays in filing appeals. The ITAT also relied on the landmark judgment in Civil Appeal No. 7654 of 2018, which emphasizes the importance of considering the overall facts and circumstances of each case while deciding on condonation of delay. The ITAT's approach was nuanced and context-specific, taking into account the unique facts and circumstances of the case. The ITAT's ruling would have significant implications for the taxpayer, as it would determine the outcome of the appeal and the taxpayer's liability for income tax.

The ITAT's decision highlights the importance of seeking professional advice and the need for taxpayers to exercise due diligence in complying with tax laws and regulations. The ITAT's ruling also underscores the need for the ITAT to exercise its discretion in a judicious and fair manner, taking into account the principles of natural justice and the doctrine of proportionality. The ITAT's approach would provide guidance for taxpayers and revenue authorities alike, highlighting the importance of considering the overall facts and circumstances of each case while deciding on condonation of delay.

Key Takeaways for Practitioners

The ITAT's judgment in this case provides valuable insights for practitioners and taxpayers. Here are some key takeaways:

1. Importance of seeking professional advice: The case highlights the importance of seeking professional advice from qualified and experienced tax consultants. Taxpayers should ensure that their consultants are up-to-date with the latest amendments and developments in tax law.

2. Due diligence in complying with tax laws: Taxpayers should exercise due diligence in complying with tax laws and regulations. This includes ensuring that all returns and appeals are filed within the stipulated timeframe and that all necessary documentation is maintained.
